ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for Jeera Rice or Ghee Rice; mapped to the broader cooked rice food product class as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Rice dishes) is the closest defensible category; no specific code exists for cumin-tempered or ghee-enriched basmati rice preparations.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.description: 1 cup cooked rice (approximately 1 standard plate serving)
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Jeera Rice or Ghee Rice in Singapore is approximately 200 g (about 1 cup cooked), as served alongside a curry or dal at an Indian restaurant or hawker stall.

nutrient_source.title: Jeera Rice / Ghee Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Jeera Rice / Ghee Rice (cr_id G00010717). All per-100g macros and micronutrients are taken from this record with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat is estimated from the ghee content (~1–2 tsp per 100g cooked rice) consistent with ICMR-NIN ghee composition data; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat are set to -1 as HPB does not report detailed fatty acid fractions for this dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Jeera Rice / Ghee Rice as a composite dish. GI estimated at 56 based on the University of Sydney GI Database value for cooked basmati rice (GI ~50–58, median 56), which is the primary carbohydrate base of this dish. The addition of ghee and cumin may marginally lower the effective GI by slowing gastric emptying; the median basmati value of 56 is used as a conservative estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (56 × 25.19 × 2.0) / 100 ≈ 28.2 for a 200 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 231 mg/100g is borderline for a hypertension-managed diet; partially offset by potassium at 327 mg/100g (Na:K ratio < 1 is favourable) and dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100g.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals managing hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g, avoid adding extra salt during cooking, and minimise ghee quantity to reduce overall caloric and saturated fat load; the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio provides partial compensation.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate at 25.19 g/100g with a medium GI of approximately 56 (basmati rice basis); dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100g moderates postprandial glycaemic response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit serving size to 150 g cooked rice, pair with a high-fibre vegetable side dish or dal to further blunt glycaemic response, and restrict ghee to 1 teaspoon per serving to avoid saturated fat-mediated impairment of insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at approximately 2.1 g/100g from ghee content; cholesterol reported as 0 mg/100g in the HPB source; dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100g is cardioprotective.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit ghee to a minimal quantity (≤1 tsp per serving) to keep saturated fat intake within recommended thresholds, leverage the fibre content for LDL-lowering benefit, and avoid adding salt to keep sodium within the 231 mg/100g baseline.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory whole spices (cumin, cinnamon, cloves, cardamom) provide polyphenol compounds; saturated fat from ghee at ~2.1 g/100g is within acceptable range.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory spice profile; ghee should be minimised to prevent saturated fat from exceeding the 5 g/100g threshold that may exacerbate systemic inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Jeera Rice (also called Ghee Rice) is a fragrant Indian rice dish made by cooking long-grain basmati rice that has been tempered in clarified butter (ghee) with whole cumin seeds (jeera) and aromatic whole spices such as cinnamon, cloves, and cardamom. The dish is a staple of Indian cuisine widely served in Singapore at Indian restaurants, hawker centres, and home kitchens, typically as an accompaniment to curries, dal, or grilled meats. The cooking process involves briefly sautéing cumin seeds and whole spices in ghee until they crackle and release their essential oils, then adding washed basmati rice and water or stock, and cooking until the grains are fluffy and separate. The result is a mildly spiced, aromatic rice with a slightly nutty flavour from the cumin and a rich, buttery mouthfeel from the ghee. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat (of which approximately 2.1 g is saturated from ghee), 25.19 g carbohydrate, 3.7 g dietary fibre, and 231 mg sodium. The fibre content from basmati rice and whole spices moderates the glycaemic response; the estimated GI is approximately 56 (medium), based on basmati rice values from the University of Sydney GI Database. Clinically, the dish is suitable for most adults in moderate portions; individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease should limit serving size and ghee quantity. The anti-inflammatory spice profile (cumin, cinnamon, cloves, cardamom) may offer additional benefit for individuals with inflammatory conditions such as rheumatoid arthritis.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010717) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The dish is a well-defined canonical Indian rice preparation with consistent composition across sources. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database basmati rice values, which is the closest approved analogous source. Saturated fat is estimated from ghee composition per ICMR-NIN data and is clearly documented.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.1 g/100g) is estimated from the ghee content of this dish using ICMR-NIN ghee composition data; the HPB source does not report saturated fat separately. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, most vitamins, phosphorus, zinc, selenium, and moisture data for this composite dish are not reported in the HPB Foodo database or ICMR-NIN as of 2026. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 by HPB; this is confirmed zero per the HPB source. 4. The input description ('roti bread brushed with butter') was incorrect for this dish and has been replaced with an accurate description of Jeera Rice / Ghee Rice. 5. GI of 56 is an analogous estimate based on cooked basmati rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources. 6. All core macros (protein_g 6.5, fat_g 4.21, carbohydrate_g 25.19) are non-zero and sourced from HPB; no zero-macro justification is required.
